Dear Mr.
Jackson:
V
On behalf of Councilman Mlchael
S.
Sands,
you seek
an opinion with respect to the councilman's responsibilities
under Government Code Section
87100,
the disqualificatlon re-
quirements of the Political Rcform Acct
Since your letter
raises no substantial questions involVing an interpretation
of
the Political Reform Act,
no formal opinion will
be, issued.
However,
1 hope the following informal cements will be helpful.
The facts as
I
understand
(ham are
as
follows:
The
Councilman is an attorney whose law practice )5 devoted almost
exclusively to the pzactice of criminal
law.
The
issue before
the City Council,
which
forms
tho, basis for Your opinion
re-
quest,
is whether
the City Council
should pass
a resolution
prohibiting City staff from destroying internal investigation
reports prepared by the city Police Department.
Internal in-
~
vestigation reports are prepared on the bass of citizen com-
plaints about Actions taken by a city police officeri
Pursu-
ant to California )dw, such trports may be discovered by a
defendant
in
a Criminal case
if
the defendant
has been arrested
for assault on a police officer and Lhe defendant is claininq
self defense.
As presented,
the conflict of interest issue requires
an examination of Government Code Section 57103 which provides,
inter alia:
Financial
Intérest.
An official has
a
financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of
SéCtion
87100
if
1t

that

financial
’

(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment
worth more than one thouscnd dollars
(51,000);
under Section alloi, several elements must be pre-
sent before a public official is required to disqualify him-
self from partipoting in a governmental decisioni
First, the
public otficial must possess a financial interest within the
meaning of Section svlolla), above.
Second, it must be @-
sonable foreseeable that the governmental decision Will have
an effect on the financial interest.
Third, the governmental
decision must materially affect the financial interest.
fourth,
the governmental decision-s effect on the financial interest
of the public official must be distinguishable from its effect
on the public generallyii/
pursuant to the facts provided in
your letter, it is doubtful whether the decision in question
will produce the effects set forth in elements three and four
above.
however, we need not reach these issues because the
first element concerning "foreseeabllity" does not appear to
be satisfied.
In Councilman sands' situation, it is not fore»
seeable that the approval or disapproval of the proposed gen-
eral resolution regarding the destruction of citizen complaint
records W111 financially affect the Councilman-s law practice.
From the facts presented, there is no basis for concluding that
the passage or defeat of the resolution will affect the number
of clients, the amount or size of fees, or the capability of
the firm to generate either more fees or clients.
In analyz-
ing the concept "foreseeably," the Commssion issued a policy
statement which provides that:
.,.the term requires the application of reason-
able judgment to assess the degree of likelihood
that the decision made or participated in will
affect a financial interest.
where the likeli-
'
hood is sufficiently great that a reasonable
person would predict or anticipate an effect on
a financial interest,
the effect of the decision
is foreseeable.Z/
It is the absence of the predictive or anticipatory
‘effects which foreclosos a conclusion that a decision on the
resolution would forcseeably affect Councilman Sands' law
y

of the hoard of Directors, Maiin Municipal water iiistrict,

A,
1975).
l/

mission, issued August, lava.

practice.
Thus, it is concluded that tho councilman may
participate in decisions related to the proposed resolution
before the Sacramento City council Law and Legislative Com-
mittee regarding the destruction of internal investigation
section reports.

Dear Commission Members:
I am writing to as}: the CcmmiCSion
for
a
legal opinion
Concerning the application of the Political Reform Act: of 1974;
as amended,
to a factual
situation existing
in the City of
Sacramento.
The
facts are
as follows:
The Sacramento city Council recently referred a proposed
resolution to the City Council Law and
Legislation Committee
for
a report.
The proposed resolution would amend the City resolution
on destruction of public records by adding a provision prohibiting
the city staff
from destroying Internal Investigation
Section
(hereinafter IISD reports preparea‘ by the city police Department.
A copy ot the proposed resolution la attached for your information,
The [IS reports are prepared when a citizen complains about
actions taken by a city police officer.
The maLcriaI
in the
repoits
15 usually obtained with the assurance that
it will
he
kept in confidence.
In accordance with the Case of Pitchess v.
superior Court,
(ll Cal.
3rd
531)
the [15 reports may be discovered
by the defendant
in a Criminal Case
if the defendant has been
arrested
for assault on
a police officer and the defendant
is
claiming
self-defense.
The records are discoverable
if they Show
a prior history of exvcssive force by the police officer,
After
the defendant makes
a Notion to discover the zocords,
an in~canvera
hearing
is conducted by the
Court to review the 115 records and
determinc which records arc material
or Will lead to the relevant
evidence.
If the Court decides that
cer‘llin records are relevant,
than the City may assert its privilege under Evidence Code Section
1040 to keep the records
from be ing
discoveredi
However,
if this
privilege is asserted by the city,
the court may give an Jnstruc~
tlon to the jury in the case that the particular police officer
involved has used excess Lve
forth on one or mni‘v cccdsicnf,
in
the pastl


Fair ?olitical Precticee Commission
May a, 1977
Page
2
Acting pursuant to Government Code Section 34090 and
Resolution No. 7s—lal, the Police Department,
\n June,
1975,
destroyed 115 files prepared prior to June, 1974.
(excepting case
files relating to pending Civil or criminal litigation and cases
involving terminations or rcsmnationsL
under Resolution 76~lal
city records more than two years old can be dehtroyed, with the
approval of the city hanger and city Attorney.
Such consent was
given for the destruction or the lid files prepared prior to June,
1974.
In the later part of 1976, however, the City Attorney
withdrew his consent to the destruction of Us records until the
matter could be further considered oy the city Council.
The resolution referred by the Council to the Law and
Legislation Cothttt‘e would impose a permanent prohibition on the
destruction of 115 records.
Howevui', it is possible that the
committee or the Council would propose that the records be
retained only for five or tcn years rather than forever.
Councilman Michael s. Sands is the Chairman of the Council
Law and Logislatian Committvo.
He is also an attorney who
specializes in criminal low.
Approximately 95 per cent of
Councilman Sanas'
law practice is in the criminal law field.
He
is a certified specialist in the practice of criminal law.
At
the present time Councilman Sands docs not accept as clients any
defendant who is accused of assault against a city of Sacramento
peace officer.
Cases of this type involving Sheriff‘s deputies
or Highway Patrol officers are accepted by Mr. sands, however.
In addition, Mr. Sands- term on the City Council will expire on
about December 1, 1977, and he has announced that he will not be
a candidate for ro-election.
It is reasonably foreseeable that
Mr. Sands will accept assault cases involving City police officers
after his present term expires.
The question being asked the Commission is whether Countilmon
sends can vote on the proposed resolution.
m: the present time the
resolution is being held in abeyance and Mr. sands has stated that
he will not vote on the issue unless he gets an opinion from the
Fair Political Practices Commission permitting him to do so.
The issue, it appears to me,
is whether Mr. sands is prohibited
from acting on the resolution by the proVisions of Government Code
Section 87100.
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision on
the resolution will have a material financial effect on Mri Sands'
law practice?
Mr. Sands thinks that it is not reasonably fore—
seeable because the effect of either approving or disapproving
the resolution cannot bc known at this line.
Tf the resolution is
approvcd and no further destruction of i'ocozds takes place, then
attorneys, including Mr. Sands, who represent criminal defendants
in this type of case would have access to more HS reports.
Over


.
r
G
9
/
Fair
Political Practices Comission
,'
May a, 1977
Page 3
a period of time they will be able to see more and more us files,
unless the city asserts its privilege under Evidence Code section
lo4oi
Because of the attorney-s ability to examine these files.
he may be bcttex able to defend his Client.
on the other hand,
if
the us files continue to he destroyed. then the criminal defense
attorney will have fewer Us files relating to the poliesotfioer
and thus fewer opportunities to defend on the basis of prior
p
excessive force by the officer.
However,
if these records continue
to be destroyed,
it is posseble that
a. court might rule that a
dismissal must be entered for the defendant on tne basislthat the
public agency has suppressed relevant evidence in the criminal case
by destroying the IIS
Filesi
Litigatiun
is presently pending against
the city on this issuei
The pending litigntlon involves the
question at whether the case of Penglc v1 Hitch,
12 cal. 3d sci, is
applicable.
If so, sanctions may be imposed upon the prosecution
and the pending criminal case may be dismissedi
Thus,
it i5 really
impossible to know at thts time the accuzatc effect of action on
the pending resolution.
Mrr Sands also believes Ihat thero is no conflict because it is
unlikely that his action, or the Council action, on the resolution
will have
the result of bringing addttional business to him.
Regardless of whether Mr. Sands is successful in defending these
cases ngoinst city police officers, other defense attorneys will
be able to use the some law ond muy be either more or less
successful than Mr,
Sands.
Thus,
while
it is reasonably foreseeable
that Mr.
S'nds will obtain Clients in these cases as
a result of
his
leaving the City Council,
it
is not
foreseeable that he would
obtain any Clients because of his action on the resolution.
In addition, it is my understanding that criminal defense
attorneys charge fees based on an estimate ot the time they will
spend on the particular CdSC.
No Contingent
fee
is Changed based
on whether the attorney wins or loses the case.
Councilmen Sands
charges fees in accordance with this practice.
In fact, the
American Bar Association Code of Professional Responsibility,
Section Ecz—zo condemns contingent fee arrangements in criminal
Cases.
Thus,
Councxbuan Sands would not receive
a greater
fee
from a defendant in n given case if he wlns that case because oi
the availability of more 115 files.
To sun up, the question asked \s whether Councilmen sands,
who will probably represent defendants in future costs involVing
assault against a City police afflcer, may vote on a pending
resolution which E have the effect of increesing his ability to
defend future Clients.
Although it is not part of this question, you should be ewsre
that there are two other attorneys on the city council who are
engaged in the general practice of law.
From tine to time they

handle criminal enses.
If you determine that Councilman Sends
has a conflict of Interest in the above factual Situation,
then we will need to inquire of yen further at to Mun degree
the other attorneys on the Cuuncil must dlsquAJify themselves
on Council matters relatlng to cxininel law.
We would appzeeinte you: reply to this inqulry at your
earllest convenience.
I)! truly

sections.conclusion: It is concluded that the councilman may participate in decisions related to the proposed resolution before the Sacramento City Council Law and Legislative Committee regarding the destruction of internal investigation section reports. In Councilman Sands' situation, it is not foreseeable that the approval or disapproval of the proposed general resolution regarding the destruction of citizen complaint records will financially affect the Councilman's law practice. From the facts presented, there is no basis for concluding that the passage or defeat of the resolution will affect the number of clients, the amount or size of fees, or the capability of the firm to generate either more fees or clients.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Councilman Sands, a criminal defense attorney, vote on a resolution that would prohibit destruction of police internal investigation reports, given that such reports could be discoverable in future criminal cases he might handle?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Councilman Sands may vote on the resolution because it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on his law practice. The first element of the disqualification test—foreseeability—is not satisfied, as there is no basis to predict the resolution will affect client numbers, fees, or his firm's revenue-generating capacity.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality issues present but document is readable. This is an informal opinion letter (not formal opinion) responding to Opinion Request 77-016. The incoming request letter is included at end of document showing full factual background. No formal CONCLUSION section heading, but conclusion clearly stated in body of response letter.

CONCLUSION: It is concluded that the councilman may participate in decisions related to the proposed resolution before the Sacramento City Council Law and Legislative Committee regarding the destruction of internal investigation section reports. In Councilman Sands' situation, it is not foreseeable that the approval or disapproval of the proposed general resolution regarding the destruction of citizen complaint records will financially affect the Councilman's law practice. From the facts presented, there is no basis for concluding that the passage or defeat of the resolution will affect the number of clients, the amount or size of fees, or the capability of the firm to generate either more fees or clients.

embedding.summary: The FPPC issued an informal opinion regarding whether Councilman Michael S. Sands must disqualify himself from voting on a proposed resolution prohibiting destruction of police internal investigation reports, given his criminal law practice. The FPPC concluded Sands may participate because the decision's financial effect on his practice is not reasonably foreseeable.
